Text: When faced with an important decision, it is wise to seek advice from more than one person. Getting different perspectives allows you to view the options from multiple angles and consider information you may not have thought of on your own. While any one person's advice could be biased or incomplete, collecting input from various sources helps paint a fuller picture. Friends and family members often see us in different lights and may focus on different priorities when offering suggestions. For example, a close friend may emphasize compatibility and shared interests over career advancement when advising about a relationship, while a parent might take a more pragmatic view. Hearing both views helps account for different ways of defining "better choice."

Text: Suspension bridges achieve their remarkable spanning capability by transferring the weight of the deck and traffic loads to high-strength cables draped in a catenary curve between tall towers, which in turn transmit the enormous compressive forces into the anchorages and foundations. Text: Neural networks learn through backpropagation, an algorithm that efficiently computes the gradient of a scalar loss function with respect to all trainable parameters in the network by applying the chain rule of differential calculus recursively from the output layer back to the input layer. During the forward pass, input data is propagated through successive layers of weighted linear transformations and nonlinear activation functions, producing a predicted output whose discrepancy from the true target is quantified by the loss function — typically cross-entropy for classification or mean squared error for regression. The backward pass computes partial derivatives of the loss with respect to each weight by propagating error signals backward through the network; the gradient at each layer is obtained by multiplying the upstream gradient from the subsequent layer by the local Jacobian of the layer's transformation. These gradients are then used by a first-order optimisation algorithm — most commonly stochastic gradient descent or its adaptive variants including Adam — to update the weights in the direction that reduces the loss.

Text: The proof of the prime number theorem is intimately connected to the analytic properties of the Riemann zeta function ζ(s) = Σn^(−s) for complex s, which encodes arithmetic information about primes through its Euler product representation ζ(s) = Π(1 − p^(−s))^(−1) over all primes p. Text: The wave-particle duality of electrons is formally reconciled within quantum mechanics through the probabilistic interpretation of the wavefunction, in which the squared modulus of the complex wavefunction amplitude at any point in space gives the probability density of detecting the particle at that location. This irreducible uncertainty is not a limitation of measurement technology but an intrinsic feature of quantum reality.

Text: Bayesian model comparison provides a principled mechanism for selecting among competing models or hypotheses that automatically penalises unnecessary complexity through the Bayesian Occam's razor effect. Models with excessive parameters are penalised because they spread their predictive probability over a larger volume of data space, resulting in lower marginal likelihood for any particular observed dataset. In machine learning, Bayesian neural networks incorporate uncertainty over network weights through posterior distributions, enabling principled uncertainty quantification in predictions — particularly valuable in safety-critical applications including medical diagnosis and autonomous systems. Text: The unification of electricity, magnetism, and optics achieved by Maxwell's equations represents one of the greatest theoretical syntheses in the history of physics. Text: Gödel's incompleteness theorems, published by Kurt Gödel in 1931, established fundamental limitations on the expressive and deductive power of formal axiomatic systems, profoundly reshaping the foundations of mathematics and the philosophy of logic. Text: The second incompleteness theorem, a strengthening of the first, asserts that no consistent formal system capable of expressing basic arithmetic can prove its own consistency. Inhibition of this pathway therefore affects multiple cellular processes beyond cholesterol synthesis, contributing to statins' pleiotropic biological effects. The magnitude of LDL lowering is dose-dependent and varies between individual statins according to their pharmacokinetic properties, lipophilicity, and relative potency at HMG-CoA reductase. Adverse effects, including myopathy and, rarely, rhabdomyolysis, may result from depletion of mevalonate pathway intermediates necessary for mitochondrial coenzyme Q10 synthesis in muscle tissue.

Text: The cardiovascular benefit of statin drugs derives primarily from their capacity to lower plasma LDL cholesterol through a well-characterised enzymatic mechanism. The consequent fall in intracellular cholesterol triggers proteolytic cleavage and nuclear translocation of SREBP-2, a sterol regulatory element-binding protein that transcriptionally activates the LDL receptor gene. Upregulated LDL receptors on the hepatocyte surface bind apolipoprotein B-100-containing particles and internalise them via clathrin-mediated endocytosis. Text: The concept of a limit is the rigorous mathematical foundation upon which calculus is constructed, providing a precise definition of the behaviour of a function as its argument approaches a particular value without necessarily reaching it. The formal epsilon-delta definition, due to Weierstrass, states that the limit of f(x) as x approaches a equals L if and only if for every ε > 0 there exists δ > 0 such that whenever 0 < |x − a| < δ, it follows that |f(x) − L| < ε. This formulation replaces the intuitive but imprecise notion of a variable becoming infinitely close to a value with a precise logical statement about the existence of tolerance intervals, eliminating the conceptual difficulties associated with infinitesimals that beset early formulations of calculus by Newton and Leibniz. Limits enable the definition of continuity — a function is continuous at a point if the limit equals the function value — and underpin the definitions of the derivative and the definite integral as limits of difference quotients and Riemann sums respectively.

Text: The distribution of prime numbers among the integers, while locally irregular and apparently erratic, exhibits a remarkable statistical regularity in the large-scale limit captured by the prime number theorem. Text: The energetics of nuclear fission are fundamentally governed by the nuclear binding energy curve, which plots binding energy per nucleon as a function of mass number. Text: Fibre optic cables transmit data by encoding information as modulated pulses of light that propagate through thin strands of highly purified glass or polymer via the principle of total internal reflection. The critical angle is determined by the ratio of cladding to core refractive indices; larger differences produce larger critical angles and a wider acceptance cone — quantified by the numerical aperture — within which light can be launched into the fibre and guided. Text: The broader mathematical and philosophical legacy of Gödel's incompleteness theorems extends into computability theory through the close connection between provability and computation established by the Church-Turing thesis. Text: The metal-oxide-semiconductor field-effect transistor, or MOSFET, has become the foundational switching element of modern digital electronics owing to its compatibility with planar fabrication processes, low power consumption in the off state, and scalability to nanometre feature sizes. Text: Acid-base reactions in aqueous solution are most comprehensively described by the Brønsted-Lowry framework, in which an acid is defined as a proton donor and a base as a proton acceptor. Text: The Fourier transform is a mathematical operation that decomposes a function of time or space into its constituent frequency components, expressing it as a continuous superposition of sinusoidal waves of varying frequency, amplitude, and phase. The inverse transform recovers the original function from its frequency representation, establishing a bijective correspondence between the time and frequency domains. This decomposition is particularly powerful because many physical systems respond linearly to sinusoidal inputs, making frequency-domain analysis natural for characterising their behaviour. Text: The Prime Number Theorem describes the asymptotic distribution of prime numbers among the positive integers, providing a profound insight into their seemingly random occurrence. It states that the number of primes less than or equal to a given large integer x, denoted by π(x), is approximately x / ln(x) (where ln is the natural logarithm). This remarkable theorem reveals that primes, though appearing irregularly, follow a predictable pattern on average. As numbers get larger, primes become less frequent, but never truly end. Text: Telomeres, repetitive nucleotide sequences located at the ends of eukaryotic chromosomes, provide crucial protection against genomic instability and degradation. Functioning as molecular caps, they prevent the chromosomes' natural ends from being recognized as double-strand breaks by DNA repair machinery, which would otherwise lead to fusion events or unwarranted degradation. Beyond this physical protective role, telomeres address the "end-replication problem" inherent in linear DNA replication, where lagging strand synthesis cannot fully copy the very end of the template. Telomerase, a reverse transcriptase, maintains telomere length by adding new repeats, thereby safeguarding genetic information and playing a critical role in cellular senescence and organismal aging.

This elaborate structure prevents paracellular passage of most hydrophilic molecules and large compounds. Beyond passive exclusion, specific efflux transporters actively pump harmful substances out of the brain, while highly regulated influx transporters facilitate essential nutrient uptake. This stringent control maintains the precise ionic and metabolic environment crucial for neuronal function, protecting the delicate neural tissue from circulating toxins and pathogens, yet posing significant challenges for drug delivery.

Text: Fiber optic cables transmit data using light through the principle of total internal reflection. Due to the refractive index difference, the light is reflected back into the core, rather than refracting out. Text: Mathematical induction is a powerful proof technique used to establish that a given statement, property, or formula holds true for all natural numbers (or any infinite sequence of discrete cases). Second, the "inductive step" assumes the statement is true for an arbitrary natural number *k* (the inductive hypothesis) and then rigorously proves that it must also be true for the next number, *k*+1. This two-part argument creates a logical chain, akin to a line of dominoes, ensuring that if the first domino falls and each subsequent domino knocks down the next, then all dominoes will fall. It rigorously extends a finite proof to an infinite domain.

Conversely, type 2 diabetes involves a combination of insulin resistance, where target cells fail to respond effectively to insulin, and a progressive dysfunction of beta cells, leading to inadequate insulin secretion relative to demand. While genetics play a role in both, type 1 is primarily immune-mediated, whereas type 2 is strongly associated with lifestyle factors like obesity and chronic inflammation.

Text: Eigenvalues play a pivotal role in linear algebra, particularly in understanding the behavior of linear transformations represented by matrices. The eigenvalue, denoted by λ, is the scalar factor by which the eigenvector is stretched or compressed. Mathematically, this relationship is expressed as *Av* = λ*v*. Eigenvalues are found by solving the characteristic equation det(*A* - λ*I*) = 0. They provide crucial insights into the intrinsic properties of a transformation, revealing its invariant directions. Applications range from diagonalizing matrices and solving systems of differential equations to principal component analysis and quantum mechanics, where they represent observable quantities.

Text: We analyze a candidate theory for the strange metal near optimal hole-doping in the cuprate superconductors. The two metals have emergent SU(2) and U(1) gauge fields respectively, and the transition is driven by the condensation of a real Higgs field, carrying a finite lattice momentum and an adjoint SU(2) gauge charge. Text: The C-metric solution of conformal gravity with a conformally coupled scalar field is presented. The solution may contain a black hole event horizon, an acceleration horizon, either of which may be cut by the conformal infinity or be hidden behind the conformal infinity. Text: We calculate the gravitational-wave spectra produced by the electroweak phase transition with TeV-scale Beyond-Standard-Model physics in the early universe. Our study captures the effect of quantum and thermal fluctuations within a non-perturbative framework. We discover a universal relation between the mean bubble separation and the strength parameter of the phase transition, which holds for a wide range of new-physics contributions. The ramifications of this result are three-fold: First, they constrain the gravitational-wave spectra resulting from heavy (TeV-scale) new physics. Second, they contribute to distinguishing heavy from light new physics directly from the gravitational-wave signature. Third, they suggest that a concerted effort of gravitational-wave observations together with collider experiments could be required to distinguish between different models of heavy new physics.

Text: Cross-lingual transfer is a leading technique for parsing low-resource languages in the absence of explicit supervision. Text: Vehicular networks will enable a range of novel applications to enhance road traffic efficiency, safety, and reduce fuel consumption. As for other cyber-physical systems, security is essential to the deployment of these applications and standardisation efforts are ongoing. In this paper, we perform a systematic security evaluation of a vehicular platooning protocol through a thorough analysis of the protocol and security standards. We tackle the complexity of the resulting model with a proof strategy based on a relation on keys. Text: We investigate threshold pion photoproduction in the framework of heavy baryon chiral perturbation theory. We give the expansion of the electric dipole amplitude $E_{0+}$ to three orders in $\mu$, the ratio of the pion to nucleon mass, and show that it is slowly converging. In contrast, we exhibit new and fastly converging low--energy theorems in the P--waves which should be used to constrain the data analysis. Text: Extreme multi-label text classification (XMTC) is a task for tagging a given text with the most relevant labels from an extremely large label set. We propose a novel deep learning method called APLC-XLNet. Our approach fine-tunes the recently released generalized autoregressive pretrained model (XLNet) to learn a dense representation for the input text. We propose Adaptive Probabilistic Label Clusters (APLC) to approximate the cross entropy loss by exploiting the unbalanced label distribution to form clusters that explicitly reduce the computational time. Our experiments, carried out on five benchmark datasets, show that our approach has achieved new state-of-the-art results on four benchmark datasets. Text: For a safe and reliable operation of the smart grid, timely detection of cyber-attacks is of critical importance. Moreover, considering smarter and more capable attackers, robust detection mechanisms are needed against a diverse range of cyber-attacks. With these purposes, we propose a robust online detection algorithm for (possibly combined) false data injection (FDI) and jamming attacks, that also provides online estimates of the unknown and time-varying attack parameters and recovered state estimates. Further, considering smarter attackers that are capable of designing stealthy attacks to prevent the detection or to increase the detection delay of the proposed algorithm, we propose additional countermeasures. Numerical studies illustrate the quick and reliable response of the proposed detection mechanisms against hybrid and stealthy cyber-attacks.

Text: The capacity region of the Multicast Cognitive Interference Channel (CIFC) is investigated. This channel consists of two independent transmitters that wish to multicast two different messages, each of them to a different set of users. In addition, one of the transmitters --commonly referred to as the cognitive transmitter-- has prior non-causal knowledge of both messages to be transmitted. Text: Gravitational-wave data analysis is rapidly absorbing techniques from deep learning, with a focus on convolutional networks and related methods that treat noisy time series as images. We pursue an alternative approach, in which waveforms are first represented as weighted sums over reduced bases (reduced-order modeling); we then train artificial neural networks to map gravitational-wave source parameters into basis coefficients. Statistical inference proceeds directly in coefficient space, where it is theoretically straightforward and computationally efficient. The neural networks also provide analytic waveform derivatives, which are useful for gradient-based sampling schemes. We demonstrate fast and accurate coefficient interpolation for the case of a four-dimensional binary-inspiral waveform family, and discuss promising applications of our framework in parameter estimation.

Text: Time series forecasting with limited data is a challenging yet critical task. While transformers have achieved outstanding performances in time series forecasting, they often require many training samples due to the large number of trainable parameters. In this paper, we propose a training technique for transformers that prepares the training windows through random sampling. As input time steps need not be consecutive, the number of distinct samples increases from linearly to combinatorially many. By breaking the temporal order, this technique also helps transformers to capture dependencies among time steps in finer granularity. We achieve competitive results compared to the state-of-the-art on real-world datasets.

This UCN-current-optimized source would dramatically improve cutting-edge UCN measurements that are currently statistically limited. Text: We study the phase diagram of the flux lines using the mapping to 2D bosons in the self-consistent-field approximation of Singwi, Tosi, Land, and Sjolander (STLS). The pair correlation function, static structure factor, interaction energy, and spectrum of the excited energies are calculated over a wide range of the parameters in this approximation. These quantities are used for studying the melting transition from the Abrikosov lattice into the entangled vortex liquid. The resulting $B-T$ phase diagram is in good agreement with the known estimates for the vortex lattice melting and the Monte Carlo simulations. We also discuss the effect of van der Waals interaction, induced by thermal fluctuations, together with the repulsion potential on the phase diagram.

Text: We present a scheme to improve the noise threshold for the fault-tolerant topological one-way computation with a constant overhead. Certain cluster states of finite size, say star clusters, are constructed with logical qubits through an efficient verification process to achieve high fidelity. Then, the star clusters are connected near-deterministically with verification to form a three-dimensional cluster state to implement the topological one-way computation. The necessary postselection for verification is localized within the star clusters, ensuring the salability of computation. This scheme works with a high error rate $ \sim 1 % $ and reasonable resources comparable to or less than those for the other fault-tolerant schemes, suggesting potentially a noise threshold higher than 5%.

We use different radiative cooling prescriptions, i.e. different cooling rates and models, and vary planet mass, orbit and disk models. Text: Optical rogue waves are rare yet extreme fluctuations in the value of an optical field. The terminology was first used in the context of an analogy between pulse propagation in optical fibre and wave group propagation on deep water, but has since been generalized to describe many other processes in optics. This paper provides an overview of this field, concentrating primarily on propagation in optical fibre systems that exhibit nonlinear breather and soliton dynamics, but also discussing other optical systems where extreme events have been reported. Although statistical features such as long-tailed probability distributions are often considered the defining feature of rogue waves, we emphasise the underlying physical processes that drive the appearance of extreme optical structures.

Text: We use accurate first principles methods to study the network dynamics of hydrogenated amorphous silicon, including the motion of hydrogen. In addition to studies of atomic dynamics in the electronic ground state, we also adopt a simple procedure to track the H dynamics in light-excited states. Consistent with recent experiments and computer simulations, we find that dihydride structures are formed for dynamics in the light-excited states, and we give explicit examples of pathways to these states. Our simulations appear to be consistent with aspects of the Staebler-Wronski effect, such as the light-induced creation of well separated dangling bonds.

Text: Recently Wilson [Ann. Appl. Probab. 14 (2004) 274--325] introduced an important new technique for lower bounding the mixing time of a Markov chain. In this paper we extend Wilson's technique to find lower bounds of the correct order for card shuffling Markov chains where at each time step a random card is picked and put at the top of the deck. Two classes of such shuffles are addressed, one where the probability that a given card is picked at a given time step depends on its identity, the so-called move-to-front scheme, and one where it depends on its position. For the move-to-front scheme, a test function that is a combination of several different eigenvectors of the transition matrix is used.

The applications range from various theorems on paradoxical decompositions of the plane, to coloring sparse set systems, results on graph chromatic number and constructions from point-set topology. Our main purpose is to demonstrate the ease and wide applicability of this method in a form accessible to anyone with a basic background in set theory and logic.

Text: Odd index pairings of $K_1$-group elements with Fredholm modules are of relevance in index theory, differential geometry and applications such as to topological insulators. For the concrete setting of operators on a Hilbert space over a lattice, it is shown how to calculate the resulting index as the signature of a suitably constructed finite-dimensional matrix, more precisely the finite volume restriction of what we call the spectral localizer. In presence of real symmetries, secondary $\mathbb{Z}_2$-invariants can be obtained as the sign of the Pfaffian of the spectral localizer. These results reconcile two complementary approaches to invariants of topological insulators.
